one_line_definition: The inherent uncertainty associated with a measurement procedure, independent of any presumed true answer
Measurement errors that are random in size and direction, amenable to statistical averaging over repeated trials
Non-random errors arising from flaws in experimental design that shift all measurements in a consistent direction
Symmetric bell-curve probability distribution parameterised by mean and standard deviation, commonly assumed for random measurement errors
Square root of the mean squared deviation from the true value; quoted as the error on a single observation
Standard deviation divided by √N; the error on the mean of N repeated measurements
Discrete probability distribution for count data; for N ≳ 5 approximates Gaussian with σ = √N
Calculation of the uncertainty on a derived quantity from the uncertainties on its measured inputs
Combining independent error contributions as the square root of the sum of squares
Average of measurements where each value is weighted by the reciprocal of its variance (1/σ²) to emphasise more precise results
Fitting a model to data by minimising the sum of squared residuals between observed and fitted values
Differences between observed data points and the values predicted by a fitted model
Probability threshold used to judge whether a discrepancy between measurement and prediction is statistically significant
The mean squared deviation of a random variable from its mean; the square of the standard deviation
The number of meaningful digits in a quoted numerical value; error should be quoted to 1–2 significant figures
